description: Food-grade HDAC inhibitor candidates for comp-007. Each compound is a dietary or GRAS-classified small molecule with documented or plausible HDAC inhibitory activity. ChEMBL IDs provided where available; compounds without ChEMBL ID are marked null.

candidates.name: Butyrate
candidates.food_source: Colonic fermentation of dietary fiber (gut-derived); also butter, ghee
candidates.gut_concentration_note: Colonic luminal concentration 0.5-2 mM from fermentable fiber; proximal colon peak; hepatic portal absorption reduces systemic exposure. Source: PMID 19197985 (Nian 2009 review).
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Estimated <5% reaches systemic circulation intact; colonocytes consume >90% as energy substrate before portal entry. Mechanistic extrapolation; no direct human PK measurement available at fiber-equivalent doses.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Zinc-chelating short-chain fatty acid; direct active-site inhibitor of class I and class II HDACs via carboxylate coordination of catalytic zinc

candidates.name: Sulforaphane
candidates.food_source: Broccoli sprouts (highest: ~73 mg/100 g fresh), broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts
candidates.gras_status: GRAS (dietary constituent; not independently GRAS-listed but broccoli-derived food ingredient)
candidates.gut_concentration_note: Peak plasma ~2-3 uM at 100 g broccoli sprout dose; gut-lumen concentration during absorption phase estimated 5-20 uM. Source: PMID 19197985.
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: High oral bioavailability (~70-80%); rapidly absorbed in proximal small intestine. Most systemic. Source: clinical PK studies cited in PMID 19197985.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Indirect: SFN metabolized via mercapturic acid pathway; active metabolite SFN-cys and SFN-N-acetylcysteine may interact with HDAC active site zinc. Mechanism is less direct than butyrate. HDAC inhibition documented in cell and PBMC studies.

candidates.name: Allyl Mercaptan
candidates.food_source: Garlic metabolite — produced from DADS and DATS by intestinal and hepatic metabolism
candidates.gras_status: GRAS (garlic-derived; allyl compounds listed GRAS 21 CFR 182.20)
candidates.gut_concentration_note: DADS is the dietary precursor; AM is the proximal metabolite in intestinal cells. Local gut concentration estimated from DADS studies at 200 uM equivalent (200 uM DADS -> AM). Source: PMID 14976134 (Druesne 2004), PMID 19197985.
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Volatile compound; substantial gut-first-pass metabolism. AM is primarily a gut-lumen and intestinal-cell metabolite; lower systemic exposure than sulforaphane. Estimated; no direct human PK measurement.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Competitive HDAC inhibitor; thiol group coordinates catalytic zinc. Most potent garlic-derived HDACi tested (92% HDAC activity inhibition at 200 uM in Caco-2 nuclear extracts; PMID 14976134). Class I/II selectivity not determined at isoform level.

candidates.name: DADS
candidates.food_source: Garlic oil (major component: ~60% of garlic oil organosulfur compounds)
candidates.gras_status: GRAS (21 CFR 182.20 — garlic and garlic components)
candidates.gut_concentration_note: At 200 uM in Caco-2 cell studies: 29% HDAC activity inhibition (nuclear extract). Source: PMID 14976134. Less potent than its metabolite AM.
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Lipid-soluble; absorbed in small intestine, converted to AM and allyl sulfenic acid. Substantial gut exposure before systemic absorption. Estimated from garlic oil PK literature.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Indirect HDACi — converted to allyl mercaptan (AM) intracellularly; AM is the active HDAC inhibitor. DADS itself also shows modest direct HDAC inhibition. HDAC inhibition confirmed in human colon cancer cells and in vivo (mice). PMID 14976134, PMID 17549389.

candidates.name: PEITC
candidates.food_source: Watercress, garden cress, papaya seeds — gluconasturtiin hydrolysis product
candidates.gras_status: Dietary constituent (watercress); not independently GRAS-listed as isolate
candidates.gut_concentration_note: Plasma peak ~1-3 uM after watercress consumption; gut-lumen concentration during absorption estimated 3-10 uM. Lower bioavailability than sulforaphane. Source: PMID 31228495 (Gupta 2019 — cell IC50 context).
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Reasonably bioavailable (~60%); similar to SFN. Absorbed in proximal small intestine. Source: isothiocyanate PK literature.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Isothiocyanate; metabolized similarly to SFN via mercapturic acid pathway. HDAC1 inhibition documented in cancer cell lines (PMID 31228495: HDAC1 protein levels reduced in vivo in colorectal model). Isoform-specific IC50 not available in ChEMBL; mechanism extrapolated from SFN analogy.

candidates.name: Caffeic Acid
candidates.food_source: Coffee (major polyphenol), blueberries, apples, eggplant, red wine, olive oil
candidates.gras_status: GRAS (naturally occurring in food; approved food additive in EU)
candidates.gut_concentration_note: After 1-2 cups coffee, plasma reaches ~0.5-2 uM; gut-lumen concentration estimated 20-100 uM (low absorption, ~33%). Source: hydroxycinnamic acid PK literature.
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Moderate absorption (~33%); most absorbed as chlorogenic acid conjugates, then hydrolyzed in colon by esterases. Significant colonic exposure. Source: Olthof et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2001.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Hydroxycinnamic acid with catechol moiety; HDAC inhibition proposed via catechol-metal chelation but no isoform-specific IC50 data in ChEMBL or primary literature. Cell-level histone hyperacetylation consistent with HDACi activity (PMID 27588384 context for related phenolics). ChEMBL 192 activities; none against HDAC1/2/3/6.

candidates.name: Ferulic Acid
candidates.food_source: Wheat bran (major source: ~0.4-0.8 mg/g), rice bran, corn, oats, tomatoes
candidates.gras_status: GRAS (FDA SCOGS, naturally occurring; rice bran and wheat bran constituent)
candidates.gut_concentration_note: After wheat bran consumption, plasma ~0.3-1 uM. Gut-lumen estimate 10-50 uM from colonic esterase release. Source: hydroxycinnamic acid PK literature.
candidates.oral_bioavailability_note: Moderate absorption (~30%); primarily as feruloyl conjugates in grain; colonic esterase releases free ferulic acid. Substantial colonic exposure. Source: Zhao & Moghadasian, Food Chem 2008.
candidates.hdac_mechanism: Hydroxycinnamic acid; shows histone H3 hyperacetylation in MCF-7 and HeLa cells (PMID 27588384), consistent with HDAC inhibition. No isoform-specific IC50 available. Similar mechanism to caffeic acid but with methoxy substitution reducing catechol metal-chelation capacity. ChEMBL 249 activities; none against HDAC targets.
